speaker: interviewer
text: --Edith Millman. Edith, what-- what would you like to tell us more about Liberation? What was it like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was depressed after Liberation. I remember meeting a friend, a girlfriend from the ghetto who survived. And after we embraced we both cried. And we didn't see any reason for living anymore. It was very strange. Because it-- also the war years, life was very intense. And you always just thought of a struggle to-- to pull your parents, to-- to help somebody. You-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --there was a reason for living. You had to live. I think if I didn't have my parents, I-- you know, and tried to look out for them. I probably would have given up. But this way, you know-- it wasn't-- Like, people say, gee, you had a will to live. I don't know if that was a will to live. It's just you are so busy to-- to live through the next day, to pull somebody through, to avoid the being discovered. And-- and-- so you knew why you were alive. You knew why you-- you wanted to live. You wanted to do something. You wanted to help. You wanted-- so maybe it's the will to live. I don't know. But then all of a sudden, when-- when the danger was gone, that was like, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like an emptiness. And I did-- I didn't have any joy of living. I just was empty. And then-- then you ask, what for? Why? Is there a reason for my survival? And I remember this friend of mine, we exchanged some pictures. And on the back of one of the pictures she wrote, let's stop asking why we survived and for what.

speaker: interviewee
text: We both know that we have to go on living so that at the end of life, we can look back and know that even in a-- even in a very small degree, we-- we contributed to a better-- a better world. I mean, she wrote it in Polish, so it's hard for me to you know, translate it like that. So-- and that kind of opened my eyes when she-- when I read it. I said yeah, you know, at the end of my life, I want to look back. And maybe I did contribute something. But it was-- it's-- it was a constant struggle of you know, looking for-- for a reason why you-- why you are alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, I came on the scholarship. My parents came in 1949. And my father was hoping to get a job with Standard Oil or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that time, he was already in his-- almost-- well, what was it? 50, 56 years old. So he was old for today's-- you know, for American jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And besides, they said that they had so many people who went-- who came from Europe, who used to work for the oil companies, that they couldn't start giving one job and not the other. So he did get a small pension. Later on he did work as-- for the Montefiore Hospital as an accountant. My-- I left-- I lost, actually, 48 members of my family, of my extended family. My-- of-- of the small group of students in the ghetto, I was the only one who survived. I-- of my class in my-- in the Jewish high school, of 35 kids, there were only two that survived. And I'm the-- one of them. I know how my grandfather died. He was a older man. And he-- he was supposed to walk to Auschwitz. They lived not far from Auschwitz, in a small town. And he had to come to a gathering place. And he walked with a friend. And this officer and my grandfather, soon after they left the house my grandfather apparently said, oh, I forgot something. And he went back to his house to get his shoe brush and shoe polish. He was always very neat. And he thought that wherever he was going, wherever they were taking him, he will need his shoe brush and shoe polish. So he went back. And two hours later, he was clubbed to death in there, because he couldn't walk fast enough. I know that one of my aunts, a very beautiful young woman, she was taken to Auschwitz with her sister who was a couple years older. And apparently, at one time she got out of her barrack way early, to stand in line, to wash up early or something. Anyway, she was caught. And some German guard or somebody said, what are you doing here, you-- oh you filthy, filthy Jewish swine, or something. And she said, if I were a filthy swine, I would be standing here so early in the morning to be able to wash up. So with that, he beat her. He clubbed her almost-- almost to death. She died the next day. And then her sister, apparently, my other aunt was-- I mean, she could-- didn't want to live anymore. And she became a musselman. And she died, you know, a few days. Musselmen is people who just give up and don't take care of themselves, don't-- so I-- my-- my cousins-- --were killed. Except for one-- well, actually, two. One is in Israel. She was a little girl. And one is in-- in Australia. The one that I tried to save, she did survive in Australia. But all my other cousins, the youngest one or so, [INAUDIBLE] two years old. My oldest one was 15 years old. They all perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your experiences, your Holocaust experiences, affected the way you raised your own children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. I think I was too overprotective. I always saw danger, thought of the worst. I-- but I-- I think, maybe, I demanded too much from them. And I-- but I wanted them to be able to-- to adjust to different situations. Because I think if I were very rigid, if I hadn't been able to adjust to different situations, I couldn't have survived. So I think I did want to inculcate that. Mostly I was just too overprotective.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to leave for your children, your grandchildren, or for the world, for that matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess that you have to-- to judge people by their actions, and by their character, not to judge anybody by their-- the color of their skin or religion or occupation. I-- I met people who are very cool. And-- but I met people who were good. And that prostitute who didn't want payment for saving my father's-- you know, hiding my father. And she endangered her own life. So even by-- by occupation or profession, just have to judge people by their character and by the way they behave. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much, Edith.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and I want to say that it really happened. So in case anybody hears that it never happened, I-- I'm still living proof of it, that it-- that it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, would you please introduce this handsome man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, this is my better half. That's Irving. And we met in 1948, over a microscope. And we studied together in Kentucky, where he was getting his master's degree. Later on we went for-- he-- we went to Chicago, where he got his PhD.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in 1949-- IRVING (HUSBAND): Sure-- --shortly after my parents came to the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irv, would you like to say something? IRVING (HUSBAND): Yeah. Well, I served in Europe, in the army. And I went all through England, France, and Germany, and even-- even Czechoslovakia. So I've seen a lot of the camps there. And I'm very proud of Edith. I think-- I think her story--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. IRVING (HUSBAND): --has to be told. Hers and all survivors. I think this sort of a thing is extremely important. Because we have deniers today, who say it never happened. And they're all going. And when they all go, the story is going to be buried. So I'm hoping that at least it's on print, on film, that people will know that it's true. So keep talking. I only encourage her.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Ready? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these are my parents and myself. The picture was taken in 1939, probably, in the winter, before the-- before the war. It was taken in Warsaw. And you're seeing me in my school uniform. We had to have navy blue uniforms. It's a winter coat. But we also had a little beret with a number on it. So that's how we could see which school you went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I think the original was hidden with a whole album of photos that a Gentile friend of ours was saving for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Who is in this photo, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me before the war, with the little dog that saved my life. His name was Tuti. And we-- we had to put him to sleep in the ghetto. That picture was taken, I think, in 1938 or '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also in the album that was saved by some friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my grandfather, my mother's father, in a German uniform during World War I. He's the man who went back for his shoe brush and shoe polish before he was taken to-- was on the way-- way to Auschwitz. And he was clubbed to death because he couldn't walk fast enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you know how long he served in the German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: And also it was in the album.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother and my aunt. My grandmother on my father's side. And my aunt, my father's oldest sister. And neither of them survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that it was taken on some vacation, somewhere where they went on vacation, probably in 19-- maybe '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, we had it in the same album.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of the girls I studied with in the ghetto. And two of the professors, the first one, I am not quite sure what his name was. I think it was Brahms. I forgot. The-- that's on-- on the left. The girl next to him is Anka. And she was the girl who escaped from the ghetto, came to-- to me. I bleached her hair. And she couldn't take the life outside of the ghetto, went back and perished. The next girl, I think, was Miriam. And she was an extremely bright girl. She perished. The next one is Helen, also perished. The next one is [? Irka, ?] Irene. She perished. The next one is Lily. And I don't know, she-- never heard from her. The next one is Maya. And the one next to Maya is-- is me. And the gentleman on the right is the professor who taught us physics and calculus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taking in the ghetto. And I don't remember if it was in 19-- '40-- end of '41 or possibly spring of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you happen to have the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had these pictures and then we had individual pictures of all the girls. And the picture was much smaller. This is a-- a blow up. And when I escaped from the ghetto, I took it with me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture of me, on the Aryan side, when I was on false papers. And it shows my bleached hair. And the reason I have it was that I-- I was giving-- somebody asked me to teach them German, a young man. And I went there for a few weeks and taught him. And then when it came time to pay, he said he didn't have any money. But he apparently was a photographer. [LAUGHS] And he's going to take my picture. So he took a few pictures of me. And that was my payment for teaching him some German.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken probably in 19-- '43, or beginning of 1944. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken near Warsaw, not in-- near Warsaw, in a small town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my student book. In-- in Poland, in 1944, when I attended medical school at the University of Maria Curie-Sklodowska, in Lublin. And the reason I'm showing it, it's still in the name of Jadwiga Olszewska. Because the first year, after liberation, I didn't admit that I was Jewish. And I didn't give my-- but I-- I changed off-- a year later I went-- well, a few months later, I went to the Jewish committee. And I proved that Olszewska is the same as Greifinger. So my other documents are already in my maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Now, these are all post war. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Never wanted to get married. Never wanted children. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Never say never.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] quickly. [LAUGHTER] Married, had a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture, taken in June of 1949. We had a very small wedding. And that was the wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in New York. Right after the ceremony, we went to a park. And then we had dinner in a restaurant with the immediate family. And that was it. OK, This picture was taken in 1993, in Akron, Ohio, when my husband was inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame, for his patent on hepatitis B. You have there my son and my daughter, and my husband with his metal.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is the wedding picture of my son, Steven and his bride, Dorianne. This picture was taken in 1990, in-- in Massachusetts, near Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter's wedding, Diane. And her bridegroom, Martin. This picture was taken in 1994, in Washington DC.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my first granddaughter-- grandson. I'm sorry. [LAUGHS] Eric, Diane, my daughter, is holding him. He was born in January in 1996, during the blizzard.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Washington DC. And it's my first grand-- grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in 1996?

speaker: interviewee
text: '96. And I am 72 years. [LAUGHTER] That's a picture of my granddaughter, Talia. She is the daughter of my son, and Steven. And she is just a few weeks old there. She was born in February, 1996. And that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that you, holding her?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's me holding her there. That's my son with a beard. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Boston.
